sub_topics.title: Needlework Tools

instructional_blocks.hook: Show learners real or picture samples of small needlework tools. Ask learners if they have seen or used any of the tools at home.
teacher_steps: Show real sewing tools (needle, thread, tailor's chalk, tape measure, thimble, pin cushion) or large picture flashcards of each tool.
Ask learners to say the names of each tool.
Place the actual sewing tools (or flashcards) on a table.
Say a tool's name and have a learner come forward and pick the correct tool.
Repeat until all learners get a turn.
Give each learner a worksheet with pictures of small needlework tools.
Ask learners to color the pictures while you move around, asking: 'What is this tool called?' Encourage them to say the name aloud.
learner_steps: Mention the names of real sewing tools given or large picture flashcards of each tool
Pick the correct tool in matching game
Color the pictures of needlework tools

teacher_steps: Provide learners with paper and pencils.
Guide them to draw and color small needlework tools.
Display their drawings on the classroom wall.
Encourage creativity - learners can color the tools afterward if time allows.
learner_steps: Draw small needle work tools (tailors chalk, tape measures, thimble…)

teacher_steps: Using the Tape Measure – Learners take turns measuring simple objects like their hands or a piece of fabric
Using Tailor's Chalk – Each child marks a line or a shape on fabric.
Using the Thimble – Show how to wear it on the finger for protection.
Using the Pin Cushion – Learner's place pins into the pin cushion and remove them carefully.
Threading a Needle (Optional for Advanced Learners) – With teacher guidance, demonstrate how to put thread through a needle's eye.
Provide each learner with a small piece of fabric and a threaded needle (with supervision).
Guide them to make a simple running stitch or just poke the needle through the fabric for practice.
Help them place pins in fabric to hold pieces together before sewing.
learner_steps: Demonstrate how to use small needlework tools: Tape Measure, Tailor's Chalk, Thimble, Pin Cushion, Threading a Needle
Make a simple running stitch or just poke the needle through the fabric for practice

teacher_steps: Display all the needlework tools on a table or mat.
Call learners one by one to pick a tool and place it in the correct storage compartment.
Encourage them to say the tool's name aloud.
Ask questions like 'Where should we keep needles?' (Answer: In a needle case).
Begin by asking questions like: 'What do you think will happen if we leave pins or needles on the floor?' 'Where do we keep tools after sewing?'
Talk briefly about why proper storage is important (safety, avoiding loss or injury, keeping tools clean and ready to use).

Demonstrate to learners how each tool should be stored: Needles and pins go into a pin cushion or needle case. Thread goes into a small box or sewing basket. Scissors should be kept in a covered container or a safe section of the box. Tape measures should be rolled neatly and stored in a drawer or box
Give learners a small box, pouch, or plastic container.
Let them arrange tools neatly inside it.
Label compartments together ('Pins,' 'Needles,' 'Thread'...).
learner_steps: Pick tools and place it in the correct storage compartment
Practice storing the tool using real or toy tools and a storage container (or picture cut-outs)
In small groups, work organize tools correctly

sub_topics.short_notes: Needlework tools help us sew and make small fabric items. Some tools help us measure, others mark fabric, and some protect our fingers while sewing. Learning to use and store these tools properly makes sewing easier and safer. Common tools include tailor's chalk, tape measure, thimble, and pin cushion. Proper storage keeps tools safe and easy to find.

vocabulary.definition: Sewing and making things with a needle and thread
A special chalk used to draw on fabric before sewing
A long strip used to measure fabric and body size
A small cap worn on a finger to protect it while sewing
A small soft pad used to keep sewing pins safely
Keeping things in a safe place when not using them

questions: Assess the ability to identify and name tools
Assess understanding of functions of tools and safety practices
Assess use of needlework tools (measuring, marking, sewing), threading a needle
Assess ability to sort and store tools
Assess practice of safety, responsibility, cleanliness, teamwork, and care for tools

sub_topics.title: Lines

teacher_steps: Start with a brief discussion about lines. Ask the pupils questions: 'what do you think a line is?'
Allow learners to draw different line on the board
Show a chart or poster with examples of different lines: Straight line, Horizontal, vertical, curved line Zigzag Wavy line dotted line
Point to different lines on the chart and ask pupils to name them
Engage learners in matching name of lines to their correct visual representation
learner_steps: Identify different lines (straight line, zig-zag, wavy, curve…)
Match lines to their correct names

teacher_steps: Demonstrate drawing of each type of line on the board using freehand, explaining how to draw them and where they might be used (road for straight lines, a snake for wavy lines…).
Guided Practice: Step-by-Step Drawing: Ask the learners to practice drawing the different types of lines in their books or on paper using freehand.
Provide verbal cues as they draw: 'Can you draw a straight line from left to right?' 'Now try drawing a wavy line, like the ocean.' 'How about a zigzag line, like the zigzag in the mountains?'
Walk around the classroom and offer help as needed. Encourage learners to use different colours for each type of line to make it visually engaging.
learner_steps: Draw different types of lines using free hand

teacher_steps: Demonstrate proper use of drawing instruments
Start with tracing tasks to help learners understand movement and alignment.
Show learners how to place the ruler flat on the paper and hold it steady with one hand.
Slowly draw a line with one hand, narrating the steps (Hold the ruler with one hand… draw along the edge)
Give hands on practice, let learner practice with real rulers in pairs or small groups.
Remind learners that neatness matters than speed
Praise carefully drawn lines even if they not perfect
learner_steps: Draw different types of lines using instruments

sub_topics.short_notes: Lines are instrumental in coming up with different design on paper or on electronic garget. Different lines carry different meaning in profile design. Through lines technocrats are able to interpret data and commutate effectively. Lines play an important role in creating and understanding designs, whether on paper or electronic devices. Each type of line has its own meaning and can be used to express different ideas in profile designs.

vocabulary.definition: a thin mark on the surface of something
refers to the visual, or things we can see
refers to exchange of information in any form

questions: Assess ability to recognize and name different types of lines
Assess ability to associate line types with their correct names
Assess ability to draw different types of lines freely and creatively
Assess ability to use instruments correctly to draw precise lines
Assess accuracy and variety in lines drawn on the board

sub_topics.title: Paper Crafts
competences: Make different Pictures using the Collage technique

teacher_steps: Briefly explain that paper craft involves creating decorative or functional items using different types of paper and folding, cutting, gluing, or molding techniques
Show learners various paper craft items. These can include: Greeting cards, Origami figures, Paper flowers, Paper baskets, Paper beads, Wall decorations, Paper fans, Envelopes, Gift boxes.
Ask learners to: Name each item. Discuss what the item could be used for. Identify the type of paper that may have been used.
learner_steps: Name and describe examples of paper craft items

teacher_steps: Recap Activity 1 briefly (what paper craft is and examples of items).
Show simple examples of paper crafts (a greeting card, a flower, a paper fan, or a small gift box...).
Demonstrate step-by-step how to make the item(s).
Use clear instructions and model each step (cutting, folding, gluing, decorating)
Guide Learners as they: Select their paper and materials, Follow steps to make their items, Decorate or personalize their items
Let learners display their items on a table, mat or classroom wall.
Invite each learner or group to briefly explain what they made.
learner_steps: Choose one or more items to make such as (Greeting card, Paper flower, Paper fan, Paper chain, Simple origami (boat, hat, bird), Paper bead bracelet or necklace…)
Display their items on a table, mat or classroom wall and describe the items made

teacher_steps: Explain what a collage is: 'A collage is a form of art where you create pictures by sticking materials such as paper, cloth, or natural objects onto a surface.'
Show sample collages (real or pictures) to inspire learners.
Briefly explain the process they'll follow: sketch → tear or cut paper → glue → finish details.
Ask learners to think of a simple picture to create (flower, animal, house, sunset, tree, person...).
Let learners lightly sketch the outline of their picture on a plain paper or cardboard.
Learners cut or tear paper into pieces of different shapes, sizes, and colors.
Guide them as they: Arrange paper pieces on the sketch. Paste each piece carefully within the right areas. Fill the whole picture space to make it neat and complete.
Encourage creativity - papers can be layered or overlapped for texture.
learner_steps: Cut out shapes from old magazines, coloured paper, or newspapers
Glue the pieces onto a larger sheet to form a picture
Add details by layering and arranging pieces creatively

sub_topics.short_notes: Paper is can be used to make many useful and decorative items. Collage is an art technique where pictures are created by sticking different materials like paper, cloth, or leaves onto a surface. Using a variety of papers, learners will design own unique collage pictures. This will help learners' express creativity while improving cutting, folding, and design skills.

vocabulary.definition: Making things by hand
Creating items using paper
A series of connected loops made from paper
A face covering used for decoration or fun
A small copy of something (e.g., house, car, train)
A picture made by sticking different materials together
The feel or look of a surface
Placing pieces on top of each other

questions: Assess ability to identify paper craft items from pictures
Assess ability to make at least one item using paper
Assess the practice of making their own paper items
Assess ability to use different materials and arrange them creatively
Assess ability to demonstrate originality and personal style in their artwork
Assess ability to express ideas visually through collage
Assess demonstration of use of colour, texture, shape, and composition creatively

sub_topics.title: Modeling

teacher_steps: Show pictures or real examples of objects to be modeled.
Demonstrate rolling, flattening, and shaping clay/plasticine into basic forms.
Let learners choose what to make, encouraging their own ideas.
Provide help as needed and allow peer sharing.
Arrange completed models for a class display or gallery.
learner_steps: Model free form objects (animals, birds, fruits, dolls…)

sub_topics.short_notes: Modeling is a fun, hands-on way for learners to create objects using clay or plasticine. By shaping animals, dolls, fruits, and birds, learners strengthen their creativity, finger strength, and imagination.

vocabulary.definition: Shaping or forming objects using clay or plasticine
A soft, natural material used for molding
A soft, colorful material for modeling

questions: Assess ability to mold clay into identifiable objects with basic form and balance
Assess ability to demonstrate creativity and safe handling of tools and materials

sub_topics.title: Letters of the Alphabet

teacher_steps: Display an alphabet chart and point to each letter while naming it.
Write a few selected letters on the board and model how to copy them.
Provide learners with dotted-line letter worksheets for tracing. (optional)
Move around the classroom offering individual support as learners copy letters into their exercise books.
learner_steps: Recognize and reproduce letters by copying

teacher_steps: Distribute printed outlines of uppercase and lowercase letters.
Demonstrate decorating one letter with different patterns (stripes, dots, spirals…).
Ask learners to choose one letter (the first letter of their name) and decorate it using crayons, markers, or colored pencils.
Display some decorated letters on the board or a 'Letter Art Wall.'
learner_steps: Design letters by decorating them creatively

teacher_steps: Demonstrate painting a large printed letter using finger paints or a brush.
Guide learners as they use paint to fill in printed letters or create their own on blank paper.
Encourage learners to use different colours for different parts of the letter.
Allow time for drying and display their painted letters in class.
learner_steps: Paint letters to develop fine motor skills and color recognition

teacher_steps: Pre-draw or print large letters for cutting practice.
Demonstrate safe scissor use and how to glue letters onto a coloured background.
Let learners decorate around their cut-out letters with stickers, shapes, or magazine cuttings.
Help learners paste letters together to form their names or familiar words like ('MOM,' 'DOG' …).
learner_steps: Use cutting and pasting to form or decorate letters

sub_topics.short_notes: Lettering is the art of drawing or designing letters in a creative way. Learners will explore how letters of the alphabet can be formed and decorated using different methods such as drawing, colouring, cutting, and pasting. Lettering helps learners to develop fine motor skills, express creativity, and communicate visually. Designing letters of the alphabet allows learners to explore creative ways of making and decorating letter forms.

vocabulary.definition: A symbol used to represent a sound in a language (A, B, C…)
Drawing or designing letters in a decorative way
A set of letters arranged in a fixed order used in a language
Making a letter by tracing or drawing it the same way
A letter shape created by cutting from paper or card
A creative plan or arrangement to make something look nice
To apply color to a surface using a brush or fingers
To make something more attractive by adding colors, shapes, or patterns

questions: Assess ability to recognize and name the letter
Assess copied letters, clearly shaped and proportional
Assess use of creative decoration techniques
Assess ability to cut and paste letters

sub_topics.title: Pool Hygiene

instructional_blocks.hook: What should we do before we swim?
teacher_steps: Begin with a question: 'What should we do before we swim?' like 'Taking a shower'
Show picture cards of showering, using the toilet, and wearing clean swimwear.
Use a story or rhyme to illustrate a child getting ready to swim properly.
learner_steps: Identify and discuss hygiene actions before swimming

teacher_steps: Set up a 'pretend' pool area in the class.
Demonstrate actions: walking to the shower, washing, drying off, using the toilet.
Invite learners in small groups to practice the routine.
Reinforce language: 'I take a shower,' 'I go to the toilet,' 'I don't pee in the pool.'
learner_steps: Act out steps to follow pool hygiene

teacher_steps: Teach a short rhyme: 'Shower first, keep it clean, Don't pee in the pool—it's mean! Go to the toilet, wash your hands, Then you're ready to splash and stand!'
Add simple movements to the rhyme for fun and memory.
learner_steps: Sing or recite a simple hygiene song or rhyme

sub_topics.short_notes: Swimming is a fun and important physical activity that helps learners develop strength, coordination, and confidence in water. In addition to learning how to swim, it is essential for learners to understand and follow hygiene rules to keep themselves and others safe and healthy in the swimming pool. Pool hygiene is important to keep the swimming pool clean and safe for everyone. Learners will explore basic hygiene rules such as taking a shower before entering the pool, using the toilet before swimming, and not urinating in the pool.

vocabulary.definition: Keeping yourself and your environment clean to stay healthy
A place filled with water where people swim
Washing the body with water before entering the pool
Free from dirt or germs
Tiny organisms that can make people sick
A place to urinate or defecate before swimming
Guidelines we follow to stay safe and healthy

questions: Assess ability to name and demonstrate pool hygiene steps
Assess ability to recognise good hygiene habits
Assess participation in role-play and discussion
Assess expression of verbal hygiene rules through songs or chants

sub_topics.title: Types of Structures

teacher_steps: Display flashcards with different structures and their names.
Discuss each structure and ask learners what they are used for.
Help learners match name cards to the correct images.
Group structures as natural or man-made using a chart or board.
learner_steps: Match pictures of structures to their names

teacher_steps: Take a walk around the school or playground.
Point out and name structures (classroom, tree, bridge, slide…).
Provide a checklist with images of structures to tick off during the walk.
Discuss what each structure is for and how it might have been built.
learner_steps: Observe and identify structures in their environment

teacher_steps: Ask learners to draw two structures: one natural and one man-made.
Let them describe their drawings in front of the class or in small groups.
Display their work on a class 'Structure Wall.'
learner_steps: Draw examples of structures they have seen

sub_topics.short_notes: Structures are objects or things that are made up of parts put together to serve a purpose. They can be found in both the natural and built environments. Learners will observe and identify different types of structures such as houses, bridges, eggs, bicycles, and cars. Learners will be introduced to various structures by observing and classifying them into natural and man-made (artificial). Through exploration, drawing, and hands-on classification activities, they will develop an understanding of how structures differ and what makes each one unique.

vocabulary.definition: Anything made of parts put together in a specific way to serve a purpose
A structure made by nature (egg, tree, nest…)
A structure made by people (house, bridge, bicycle…)
A structure built to allow people or vehicles to cross over something
A building where people live
A vehicle with two wheels used for transport
A vehicle used for transporting people or goods
A natural structure that holds a developing bird or reptile

questions: Assess ability to recognize and name various types of structures
Assess ability to classify structures as natural or man-made
Assess participation in activities such as drawing very basic structures
Assess communication when describing structures

sub_topics.title: Entrepreneur
competences: Demonstrate knowledge on the roles of an entrepreneur

instructional_blocks.hook: Have you ever bought bread from a shop or taken a ride in a taxi? The people who run these businesses are called entrepreneurs. Today, we'll learn how they help our community by making, selling, and providing services!
teacher_steps: Show pictures of local entrepreneurs (shopkeeper, tailor, driver, hairdresser…).
Discuss what each person does and where they work.
Ask learners to share if they know anyone who owns a shop, sells at the market, or drives a taxi.
Create a community chart with pictures or drawings of these entrepreneurs.
learner_steps: Identify different types of entrepreneurs in their environment

teacher_steps: Read or tell a story about a local business person.
Ask questions like: 'What did the entrepreneur sell or make?' 'How did they help the community?'
Write or draw the answers together on the board ('Makes clothes,' 'Sells food,' 'Drives people' …).
Introduce terms like goods and services and give examples.
learner_steps: Discuss the roles and importance of entrepreneurs

teacher_steps: Set up a classroom 'market' or 'shop' with toy money and goods.
Assign roles: shopkeeper, customer, driver, etc.
Guide learners to practice simple dialogues
Switch roles and let everyone have a turn.
learner_steps: Act out buying and selling scenarios

sub_topics.short_notes: Entrepreneurship is the act of creating and managing a business to make goods or provide services for the community. Learners will be introduced to the concept of an entrepreneur and the important role they play in society. By identifying local entrepreneurs and participating in activities such as role playing, learners begin to understand how goods and services are made, bought, and sold, and how these contribute to everyday life. Learners explore what it means to be an entrepreneur by observing and talking about familiar community workers.

vocabulary.definition: A person who starts and runs a business to make or sell goods or provide services
Items that are made or sold, like clothes or food
Helpful actions provided by people, like cutting hair or transporting people
A person who owns or runs a shop
A person who sells things at a market
A person who helps people or goods move from one place to another
Giving something in exchange for money
Getting something by paying money

questions: Assess ability to recognize and name different types of entrepreneurs
Assess ability to describe goods and services provided
Assess participation actively in role play and classroom market
Assess ability to communicates ideas clearly through drawing and storytelling
